title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in South Korea — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Cafés are social hubs, not just for coffee
items.text: South Koreans spend hours in cafés studying, working, or socializing—it's normal to nurse one coffee for 3+ hours. Most cafés have free WiFi and outlets, making them de facto co-working spaces.

items.label: Americano [아메리카노]
items.text: The default coffee order, usually around 3,000-5,000 won. It's stronger and cheaper than lattes, making it the everyday choice for locals.

items.label: Convenience store coffee is surprisingly good
items.text: GS25, CU, and Emart24 sell decent drip coffee for 1,500-2,500 won—perfectly serviceable if you're on a budget or in a rush.

items.label: Hongdae & Gangnam for trendy cafés
items.text: These neighborhoods have Instagram-worthy independent cafés with specialty coffee, but expect to pay 6,000-10,000 won per drink. Tourist areas near palaces charge premiums.

items.label: Bibimbap [비빔밥]
items.text: Mixed rice topped with vegetables, meat, egg, and spicy gochujang sauce—stir it all together before eating. It's healthy, filling, and available everywhere from street stalls to restaurants.

items.label: Korean BBQ [한우구이]
items.text: Grill meat at your table—beef (hanwoo) is premium but pork (dwaeji) is affordable and equally satisfying. Always order side dishes (banchan) which come free and include kimchi, pickled vegetables, and more.

items.label: Kimchi Jjigae [김치찌개]
items.text: Spicy kimchi stew with pork, tofu, and vegetables—deeply comforting and cheap (8,000-12,000 won). Perfect for cold days and a true taste of home cooking.

items.label: Tteokbokki [떡볶이]
items.text: Chewy rice cakes in spicy red sauce with fish cakes and vegetables—a beloved street food for 4,000-6,000 won. Highly addictive and often customizable with extra toppings.

items.label: Jjamppong [짬뽕]
items.text: Spicy seafood noodle soup bursting with shrimp, squid, and mussels—order with caution if you can't handle heat. Usually 12,000-15,000 won and worth every penny.

items.label: Kalguksu [칼국수]
items.text: Hand-cut noodles in a savory broth with vegetables and seafood—lighter than jjamppong but equally satisfying. A comforting lunch dish around 8,000-10,000 won.

items.label: Hotteok [호떡]
items.text: Sweet Korean pancake filled with brown sugar, cinnamon, and sometimes nuts—sold at street carts for 3,000-4,000 won. Best eaten warm and perfect as a snack or dessert.

items.label: Pojangmacha [포장마차] street tents
items.text: Informal outdoor food stalls serving tteokbokki, hotteok, and street snacks—super cheap (3,000-8,000 won) and where locals actually eat. Most active in evenings and near subway stations.

items.label: Sikdangs [식당] for authentic meals
items.text: No-frills Korean restaurants with plastic chairs and laminated menus—expect honest, delicious food at fair prices (8,000-15,000 won). These are where Korean families eat, not tourists.

items.label: Department store food courts
items.text: Higher prices (12,000-20,000 won) but very clean and reliable; great if you're overwhelmed by options or need a quick sit-down meal without language barriers.

items.label: Avoid restaurants in major tourist zones
items.text: Myeongdong, Insadong, and palace areas inflate prices by 30-50% and serve diluted versions of authentic dishes. Venture 2-3 blocks away for better value.

items.label: Night markets like Namdaemun
items.text: Bustling traditional markets with hundreds of food stalls—perfect for sampling multiple dishes cheaply and seeing how locals shop and eat.

items.label: Most menus have photos—use them
items.text: Point at pictures or use your phone's camera translator app (Papago or Google Translate) to photograph menu text. English menus often mistranslate dish names, so photos are more reliable.

items.label: Spice levels matter—ask before ordering
items.text: If you can't handle heat, explicitly ask '맵지 않게 해주세요' (maep-ji an-ke hae-ju-se-yo, 'not spicy please'). Many Korean dishes default to fiery, and there's no shame in requesting mild.

items.label: Banchan [반찬] are free side dishes
items.text: Kimchi, pickled vegetables, and other sides arrive automatically—it's part of the meal, not something to order. You can ask for refills of popular ones.

items.label: Portion sizes are generous
items.text: One dish often feeds 1.5-2 people, especially soups and stews. Share or expect leftovers—restaurants freely pack extras to-go (no judgment).

items.label: Always check prices before ordering
items.text: Korean restaurants rarely have menu English but prices are always in won—watch out for 'special' or premium versions costing 2-3x more. High-end hanwoo beef can easily be 50,000+ won per serving.

items.label: Soju [소주]
items.text: Clear, high-proof Korean spirit (16-20% ABV)—cheap, smooth, and the default drink for social occasions. Expect 2,500-5,000 won per bottle at bars; it's often paired with fried food (chimaek culture).

items.label: Beer [맥주] and Soju bombs [소맥]
items.text: Local beers like Cass and Hite are crisp and affordable (4,000-6,000 won). Mixing beer and soju ('soju bomb') is a popular drinking game—fun but dangerous for your head the next day.

items.label: Makgeolli [막걸리]
items.text: Sweet, milky rice wine (6-8% ABV) traditionally served in bowls—nostalgic and less harsh than soju. Trendy makgeolli bars have emerged, but street pojangmacha versions are authentic and cheaper.

items.label: Pojangmacha for drinking culture
items.text: Street tent bars serve soju and beer with fried snacks (tteokbokki, hotteok, fried chicken)—this is where Koreans drink and socialize. Expect 15,000-30,000 won per person for drinks and food.

items.label: Chimaek [치맥] is fried chicken + beer
items.text: A beloved pairing: crispy, spicy fried chicken (치킨) with cold beer (맥주). Restaurants and pojangmacha focus on this—it's casual, affordable (15,000-25,000 won), and authentically Korean.

items.label: Respectful drinking customs matter
items.text: When older people pour drinks, accept with both hands and turn slightly away when drinking. Never pour your own drink if others are present—it's considered rude. Always pour for others first.

items.label: Wait for the eldest person to start eating
items.text: In formal settings or with Korean families, wait for the oldest person to pick up their spoon before you start. This shows respect and is deeply ingrained in Korean culture.

items.label: Use spoons for rice and soup, chopsticks for side dishes
items.text: Korean etiquette specifies spoons for rice (not chopsticks), chopsticks for banchan and meat. Metal utensils are traditional and standard in most restaurants.

items.label: Slurping noodles is acceptable and even encouraged
items.text: Unlike some cultures, loud slurping shows you're enjoying the food. It's normal behavior in Korean restaurants.

items.label: Never pour your own drink—pour for others instead
items.text: At meals and drinks, hold the bottle with both hands and offer to pour for others first. They'll reciprocate, creating a social rhythm. Pouring your own is seen as selfish.

items.label: Tipping is not customary and not expected
items.text: Unlike the US, tipping in South Korea is rare and can even be insulting. Prices shown are final, and service charges are sometimes included in bills at upscale restaurants.

items.label: Finish your rice as a sign of respect
items.text: Leaving rice uneaten is considered wasteful and disrespectful to the cook, especially in traditional or family settings. Always finish or ask for a small portion if you can't eat much.

items.label: Payment etiquette: one person pays, or split evenly
items.text: It's common for the oldest or most senior person to pay, or everyone splits the bill exactly (no itemizing). Offering to pay individually is rare and can create awkwardness.

items.label: Learn basic food phrases
items.text: Key phrases: '맛있어요!' (mat-iseo-yo, 'delicious!'), '물 주세요' (mul ju-se-yo, 'water please'), '계산서 주세요' (gye-san-seo ju-se-yo, 'bill please'). Koreans appreciate effort and are forgiving of mistakes.

items.label: Download a translation app
items.text: Papago (Korean-native) or Google Translate's camera feature are lifesavers for reading menus and understanding prices. Most restaurants lack English, so these tools are essential.

items.label: Allergen warnings: tell staff immediately
items.text: If you have allergies, write them down or use your phone to show them clearly. Many dishes contain hidden sesame, shellfish, or soy, so don't assume—always confirm.

items.label: Cash is still king at street food and small restaurants
items.text: While cards are accepted everywhere, many pojangmacha and small sikdangs are cash-only. Keep 10,000-50,000 won notes handy.
